title: Chapter 23: The Summons
content: The federal indictment arrived on a Thursday afternoon.

Chloe was at The Coast when Elena called. She was standing in what used to be the Isolation Wing, now stripped down to concrete and exposed pipes. The demolition crew had finished the day before. The room that had held her for seventy-two hours was now a shell waiting to become something else.
Her phone buzzed. Elena's name.
"I have news," Elena said.
"Good or bad?"
"Good. Your father has been indicted."
Chloe sat down on a stack of drywall sheets. "Indicted for what?"
"Fraud. Conspiracy. False imprisonment. The federal prosecutor picked up the case this morning. They've been building it since the hearing."
"That's fast."

"The evidence package Mia put together was comprehensive. The email chain from the data broker. Jake's testimony. The financial records. The shell company documents. The prosecutor told me it was the most organized case package she had ever received from a civilian."
Chloe looked at the bare walls of the former Isolation Wing. The walls she had scratched at with her fingernails. The walls that had witnessed her at her lowest.
"When is the arrest?"
"It's not an arrest yet. He's been summoned to appear before the grand jury next week. If they indict, the arrest follows."
"He's going to fight it."
"He's going to try. But the evidence is overwhelming."
Chloe was quiet for a moment.
"How do you feel?" Elena asked.

Chloe looked at the room. The room that had been designed to break her. The room that was now going to become a yoga studio, because she had decided that was the most disrespectful thing she could do to it.
"I feel like I won," she said.
Elena was quiet for a moment. Then she said: "You did."
Chloe stayed at The Coast for the rest of the day.
She walked through the building as the construction crew worked around her. The sound of drills. The smell of fresh drywall. The sight of sunlight pouring through windows that had once been barred.
She found Amara in the new group therapy room, supervising the installation of a whiteboard.
"Your father was arrested?" Amara asked.
"Indicted. The arrest comes next week if the grand jury votes to proceed."
"They will. The evidence is solid."
"I know."
"Does it feel good?"

Chloe leaned against the doorframe and watched a worker mount the whiteboard to the wall. "It feels strange. I spent so long wanting this. Now that it's happening, I don't know what to do with myself."
"That's normal," Amara said. "You've been in fight mode for months. Your body doesn't know how to stop."
"How do you stop?"
"You don't all at once. You learn. One day at a time."
Chloe looked at the room. The room where patients would sit in a circle and talk about their lives. The room where healing would happen.
"I think I can learn that," she said.
Chloe walked through the rest of The Coast before she left. She stopped in every room. The future therapy rooms. The future dorms. The future kitchen. Every empty space was a possibility.
She thought about the women who would fill these rooms. The ones who had been told they were too much, too loud, too difficult. The ones who had been sent away for refusing to shrink themselves.
She thought about the Manifesto.
*Rule 12: You are not the problem. The system that tried to fix you is the problem.*
She wrote it on a sticky note and stuck it to the wall of what would become the common room.
Then she walked out the front door and into the sunlight.
That night, Chloe sat alone in Elena's guest room, reading the indictment document on her phone.
It was sixty-seven pages long. She did not read every word. She read the parts that mattered.

*Count 1: Conspiracy to Commit Fraud.*
*Count 2: False Imprisonment.*
*Count 3: Healthcare Fraud.*
*Count 4: Money Laundering.*
Each count carried a maximum sentence of twenty years.
Her father was looking at eighty years.
She put the phone down and stared at the ceiling.
Eighty years. Her father would die in prison.
She had known this was coming. She had prepared for it. She had built the case that made it possible. But seeing it in writing was different.
She picked up the phone again. Scrolled to her contacts. Her mother's name was still there.
She stared at it for a long time.
Then she locked the phone and set it on the nightstand.
Not tonight.
Tonight, she was going to sleep.
And tomorrow, she was going to keep building.
